comments:
Summary
This paper addresses SVM classification when the input similarity matrix is indefinite (not positive semidefinite). The authors formulate a penalized kernel-learning problem that enforces the learned proxy kernel to be close (in Frobenius norm) to the given indefinite matrix while constrained to be PSD. A key technical result is that the inner minimization over the kernel has a closed-form solution: the PSD projection of a rank-one update of the input matrix, K* = (K0 + (Yα)(Yα)^T/(4ρ))+. This reduces the overall problem to a concave (but nondifferentiable) eigenvalue optimization in α. The paper presents algorithms (projected gradient with efficient rank-one eigen-updates, analytic-center cutting-plane method) and relates the work to a SIQCLP/exchange method. Extensions to SVR, one-class SVM, Mercer kernels and weighted (componentwise) penalties are given. Numerical experiments on USPS and UCI data compare the method to simple spectral fixes (thresholding/flipping/shifting), a modified SVM, direct (nonconvex) SVM, and an SIQCLP implementation.
Strengths
- Clear, simple and practically useful formulation which convexifies the problem by learning a nearby PSD proxy kernel.
- The closed-form inner solution (projection of a rank-one update) is elegant and leads to algorithms that exploit efficient rank-one eigen-updates, reducing per-iteration cost.
- Good range of extensions (SVR, one-class, handling Mercer kernels, componentwise penalties) that show the approach is broadly applicable.
- Reasonable experimental evaluation comparing to common spectral heuristics and showing cases where the method helps; code availability is mentioned.
- Sound convexity arguments and derivations; the projection-based solution is mathematically clean.
Weaknesses and suggestions
- Experiments are somewhat limited in scale and scope. All experiments are on moderate-size benchmark sets; larger-scale evaluations (or discussion of practical limits) would strengthen claims about scalability. In particular, for very large n the O(n^2) per-iteration cost and initial eigen-decomposition cost may become prohibitive; discussion of low-rank approximations or sampling would be useful.
- Selection and interpretation of the penalty parameter ρ is critical in practice. The manuscript would benefit from more guidance on selecting ρ (beyond cross-validation), analysis of sensitivity to ρ, and discussion of how to interpret extreme regimes (very small or very large ρ) for practitioners.
- The heuristic for transforming the test kernel (thresholding negatives after updating training block) is plausible but only briefly described; more experiments or discussion about generalization guarantees when applying such test-time transformations would help.
- While comparisons to spectral fixes and to the SIQCLP method are included, comparison to pseudo-Euclidean embedding approaches (pE-space SVMs) and other recent methods for indefinite kernels could be expanded in experiments.
- Some numerical details (choice of step sizes for the projected gradient, smoothing parameter ϵ, and stopping tolerances) are given as heuristics; clearer practical recommendations would aid reproducibility.
Minor
- A few portions of the provided text include duplicated/garbled lines (likely an artifact of this copy) but overall the exposition is clear.
Overall assessment
This paper makes a meaningful contribution to the problem of learning with indefinite similarity matrices by offering an elegant convexified formulation with an explicit inner solution and practical algorithms leveraging rank-one updates. The theoretical development is sound and the experiments show promise. With more extensive empirical validation and additional practical guidance (on ρ selection, scaling strategies), the work would be even more convincing and broadly useful. I recommend acceptance (strong recommendation).
